stem: On a deep circuit run on real hardware, you raise the Estimator `resilience_level` from 0 to 2. What is the expected effect?

options.text: Jobs finish faster, because mitigation lets the estimator use fewer shots.
No change in runtime; resilience level only selects which physical qubits are used.
More accurate (less biased) expectation values, at the cost of longer runtime and more QPU usage.
The circuit is automatically simplified to fewer gates before execution.

explanation.correct: Higher resilience levels build more resilience against errors by running and post-processing larger ensembles of circuits (e.g. ZNE's noise-amplified copies at level 2). This yields more accurate, less biased expectation values but takes longer and consumes more QPU time — a cost/accuracy trade-off.

distractors.A: Mitigation adds overhead (extra circuits and shots), so higher levels are slower, not faster.
distractors.B: Resilience level controls error mitigation, not qubit selection, and it clearly changes runtime.
distractors.D: Gate-count reduction is the transpiler's job (optimization_level); resilience level does not simplify the circuit.
